title: What Tension Is Trying to Tell You

hook.rwenLine: Chronic tension is not a flaw to fix — it's information you haven't listened to yet. Today we treat the body as a messenger.
hook.culturalNote: Bessel van der Kolk on the body keeping the score: emotional content that isn't processed verbally tends to express itself somatically. The tight shoulder is often a story you haven't told yet.

chunks.native: Tension carries information about what you've been carrying — emotionally, relationally, practically
chunks.literal: Somatic experiencing (Levine); van der Kolk on trauma and the body

chunks.native: Not 'how do I get rid of this tension?' — but 'what is this tension about?'
chunks.literal: Internal Family Systems (Schwartz) — parts carry burdens
chunks.phonetic: The answer is usually closer than you think.

chunks.native: Shoulders carry responsibility. Jaw carries swallowed words. Stomach carries fear. Lower back carries the loads you should have set down.
chunks.literal: Pattern observations from somatic clinicians — useful heuristics, not diagnostic claims

pattern.name: Ask the tension what it's about.
pattern.explanation: Pick the tightest spot. Put your attention there. Don't try to release it. Ask: 'what are you about?' Wait. Often the answer comes — a person, a decision, an unsaid thing. Sometimes it doesn't. Either way you've listened, which the tension wasn't getting before.

examples.native: What you've been responsible for that nobody else is sharing
Something you wanted to say today and didn't

examples.target: Knot in the stomach before Mondays
examples.native: Something about the week ahead you've been avoiding looking at

exercises.question: Your jaw is clenched. Best move?

options.text: Put attention there and ask what it's about. Wait.

exercises.explanation: Forced release is temporary. Listening to what the body is carrying often lets it set the load down on its own.

correct: What has my neck been carrying this week?

exercises.sentence: Don't ask how to get rid of the tension. Ask what it's _____ .

exercises.question: You ask your tight shoulders what they're about. Nothing comes. What do you do?

options.text: Try again tomorrow. Sometimes the answer needs time. The listening itself is the practice.

exercises.explanation: The listening is the work. Sometimes the answer surfaces hours later. Sometimes a few days. The body has been talking for a while; you're just starting to listen.

rwenDialogue.intro: Pick the tightest spot right now. Where is it?

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Now put your attention on it for thirty seconds. Don't try to relax it. Just notice. Ask: 'what is this about?'

userChoices.target: Shoulders — work. I've been doing two people's jobs.
userChoices.feedback: There it is. The shoulders weren't wrong. The question now is what to do with what you heard.

userChoices.target: Jaw — the conversation I keep not having with my sister.
userChoices.feedback: Useful. The jaw has been holding what your mouth hasn't said. That's data.
Fine. The listening is the practice. Try again tomorrow.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: The body has been talking. We're learning to hear it.

prompts.prompt: What does the jaw often carry?

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.task: Find your tightest spot today. Put attention there for one minute. Ask: 'what are you about?' Write down whatever surfaces — even if it's nothing. Especially if it's nothing.
mission.rwenSignoff: Tension is information you haven't yet heard.

phase8.scenario: User has identified their tightest spot and asked the question. Rwen helps them sit with whatever surfaced — or with the absence.
phase8.rwenRole: Calm, patient companion. Not interpreting; helping the user listen.
phase8.successCriteria: User has named the spot, the question they asked, and what (if anything) surfaced.
